name: Computer Networks 3
description: Protocols, architectures and layers of computer networks.

questions.question: Which of these media is known for being natively immune to electromagnetic interference?
options: Terrestrial radio, since the signal travels freely through the air.
Coaxial cable, due to the strong shielding mesh in its jacket.
Optical fiber, since it carries data using pure light.
Twisted-pair copper that dissipates any electrical noise.

questions.question: Which of the following transport protocols would be ideal for video streaming where losses are acceptable but low latency is critical?
options: TCP, since it offers ordered and error-free delivery.
UDP, since it avoids the delay of building slow connections.
DNS, due to its extremely high speed when discovering IPs.
Plain IP, since it guarantees fast delivery to the physical layer.

questions.question: Distinct from interception tactics, what exactly does a criminal do during an IP 'spoofing' attack?
options: The unauthorized reading of confidential data inside the corporate network.
The deliberate overloading of servers seeking to take down external access.
The deletion of electronic signatures aiming to bypass access rules.
The injection of malicious packets into the network with a forged source address.

questions.question: When using the Transport-layer Sockets API, what is mandatorily required besides the IP to deliver data to the correct process?
options: The MAC address physically burned into that host.
The official encryption key that was sent to the local router.
The connection port number tied to that destination process.
The size in bits of the transport header contained in the segment.

questions.question: Which component of the DNS hierarchy is directly responsible for generic domains such as .com, .org, .net and country domains such as .br?
options: Local DNS Servers
Root DNS Servers
Authoritative DNS Servers
TLD (Top-Level Domain) Servers

questions.question: What is the main operational characteristic of a 'sniffing' attack?
options: The injection of packets into the network carrying a forged source address.
The silent and criminal reading of packets traveling across that network.
The intentional overloading of that resource to make it inoperative.
The secret alteration of digital signatures to corrupt data.

questions.question: In the routine of packet switching, what essentially triggers the loss of a packet as it passes through the router?
options: The arrival of a packet at a router whose output buffer is already full.
Collision of several electrical pulses traveling simultaneously on the cable.
The detection of cyber threats natively blocked by its switch.
The major incompatibility between the remote sender and the destination machine.

questions.question: What is the primary purpose of using the 'conditional GET' request?
options: To guarantee the secure sending of passwords placed in the main body of the request.
To request only the header of a response, excluding the body of the document.
To completely replace an existing file on the server with the uploaded version.
To prevent objects from being sent if the browser already has a current version in cache.

questions.question: In packet networks, what is the correct definition of 'propagation delay'?
options: It is the time the router spends checking for errors
It is the time the packet waits stuck in the output queue
It is the time to push all the bits onto the physical link
It is the time the signal takes to travel the entire link

questions.question: What fundamentally characterizes the mechanical behavior of a non-persistent HTTP connection?
options: The link closes its own connection right after transmitting a single object.
The host issues twenty-two parallel requests during a browsing session.
The central node ignores all encryption in order to act faster.
The system skips every mandatory handshake aiming for a smooth flow.
